summary: I was born in 1976 right here in Harlan County, at Appalachian Regional Hospital in Harlan. I've lived most of my life in this area, particularly in Cumberland, which I consider home. Harlan County has a rich coal mining history, with places like Benham and Mary Helen playing important roles. My family, including my dad and uncle, were deeply involved in the coal industry here, which shaped much of my upbringing and knowledge.

Benham is a significant coal camp in Harlan County where my dad worked as a coal miner for International Harvester. He started as a general laborer and eventually became the safety director before the mine shuttered. The closing of Benham's mine was a big event in the community, marking the end of an era for the town and the coal industry there. I learned a lot about Benham from my dad and uncle, who shared stories about its importance locally and regionally.

Wisconsin Steel was one of the coal companies operating in Harlan County, alongside International Harvester and Benham Coal Incorporated. My family had close ties to these companies through my dad's work. When Wisconsin Steel and the other companies shuttered, it marked a significant shift in the local economy and community. My knowledge about Wisconsin Steel comes from the stories my dad and uncle shared about its operations and impact.

International Harvester was the coal company in Benham where my dad worked for many years. He started as a laborer and rose to become the safety director before the mine closed. The company played a central role in the community, and its closing was a major event that affected many families, including mine. I gained much of my knowledge about International Harvester from my dad and uncle, who knew the inner workings of the company well.

I was born at Appalachian Regional Hospital in Harlan in 1976, so it holds a special place in my life story. This hospital has served as a key healthcare facility for people in Harlan County and the surrounding areas. Being born there connects me to the community from the very start. It’s part of the fabric of life in this region.

Cumberland is where I grew up and still live today; it’s truly home for me. Although I was born in Harlan, my family settled in Cumberland, and I attended school nearby. My dad worked in coal mines around the area, and my mom is from Lynch, so Cumberland sits at the heart of my family’s life. It’s a small community with strong ties to the coal industry and local culture.

entity: Southeast Kentucky Community and Technical College
summary: I earned my associate degree from Southeast Kentucky Community and Technical College before continuing my education. While working there, I served as the agricultural services manager and was involved in community development projects, including building an aquaculture learning center in Lynch. This college was an important stepping stone in my career and helped me stay connected to the region’s agricultural and educational needs.

I completed my bachelor's degree in agriculture, animal science, at the University of Kentucky. UK is a prominent institution in the state and played a big role in shaping my knowledge and career path. After graduating, I eventually returned to Harlan County to work with the UK Cooperative Extension Service, which allowed me to serve my home community. The university’s influence is strong in Kentucky, and I’m proud to be an alumnus.

I attended Victory Road Christian Academy from grades seven through twelve. It was a small private school located in Blair, just outside Cumberland city limits. The school was an important part of my youth, providing a close-knit educational environment. Unlike public schools in Benham or Lynch, it was independent and didn’t receive support from the coal companies.

Blair is a small community just outside of Cumberland where I went to school at Victory Road Christian Academy. My dad spent much of his childhood years in Blair before working in the coal mines. It’s part of the broader Harlan County area and has close ties to the coal mining history of the region. Blair is a place that connects my family’s past with my own experiences growing up.

Lynch is where my mom grew up and where I have family roots, so I often say I’m half Lynch. It’s another coal camp town in Harlan County with a rich mining history. I also worked on projects in Lynch, like the aquaculture learning center located in a former US Steel building. Lynch has been important both personally and professionally in my life.

After International Harvester in Benham closed, my dad found work at Great Western Resources, which operated New Horizon Coal in the Mary Helen community. He continued his coal mining career there, moving from labor to management roles. Great Western Resources was part of the later chapter of coal mining in Harlan County for my family. It helped sustain us after the older mines shuttered.

New Horizon Coal was the mining operation in Mary Helen where my dad worked after the closure of the Benham mines. It was operated by Great Western Resources and represented the continuation of coal mining in the region. My dad’s experience there included supervisory roles, and it was part of the family’s ongoing connection to the coal industry. New Horizon Coal was important in sustaining local mining jobs during that period.

Mary Helen is a community in Harlan County where my dad worked at New Horizon Coal after the Benham mines closed. It’s part of the coal mining landscape of the region and was home to later mining operations. My family’s ties to Mary Helen reflect the shifts in the coal industry over time. It’s another place that connects my personal history to the broader story of Harlan County mining.

US Steel was another major coal company in the area, and I worked on a project at a former US Steel shop building in Lynch. We converted the building into an aquaculture learning center where we raised trout using water from Mine Number Thirty. US Steel’s legacy remains in the infrastructure and history of the region, even after the mines closed. It’s part of the industrial heritage that shaped Harlan County.

When asked about my favorite singer in high school, I couldn’t really pick one, but I know that Elvis Presley was a favorite for many around my time. He was a big influence on the music scene during my youth, though I personally didn’t have a particular favorite. Elvis and Johnny Mathis were popular singers during that era, representing the music culture of the time.

Johnny Mathis was one of the singers I remember being popular during my youth, alongside Elvis Presley. While I didn’t have a favorite song, Mathis’s music was part of the soundtrack of that time. His style contrasted with Elvis but was equally beloved by many. These artists shaped the musical backdrop of my high school years.

Raymond Williams is my uncle and a key source of my knowledge about the coal industry, especially International Harvester and Wisconsin Steel. Along with my dad, he shared stories about the inner workings of the mines and the impact on the community. His insights helped me understand the history and significance of coal mining in Harlan County. I’m grateful for the knowledge he passed down to me.

I began working at the UK Cooperative Extension Service in February 2001 after serving as the agricultural services manager at Southeast Kentucky Community and Technical College. The Extension Service has been a great way for me to serve Harlan County, providing programs in agriculture, homemaking, and youth development like 4-H. Over the years, we’ve adapted to new technologies but continue traditional programs like canning and quilting. It’s rewarding to help the community maintain cultural and agricultural knowledge.

Lynch is a coal camp town where my mom grew up and where I have family roots. I also worked on community development projects there, such as the aquaculture learning center housed in a former US Steel building. Lynch represents both my family history and my professional efforts to support local agriculture and education. It’s a place that connects past and present in Harlan County.

Mine Number Thirty is a former coal mine whose water we used in an aquaculture learning center project in Lynch. We raised trout there as part of educational and community development efforts. This project repurposed a former US Steel shop building and connected the mining legacy to new uses in the community. Mine Number Thirty’s water source was essential to the success of this initiative.

4-H is a vital youth development program here in Harlan County that we support through the UK Cooperative Extension Service. It teaches leadership, gardening, and practical skills like hatching chicks and growing tomato plants. We have clubs in schools and the community, helping young people learn responsibility and skills that connect them to agriculture and their heritage. 4-H remains strong and important in the county’s social fabric.

Kentucky is my home state, and I’ve spent my life living and working here, especially in Harlan County. The University of Kentucky and Southeast Kentucky Community and Technical College have been important educational institutions for me. Kentucky’s coal mining history, rural culture, and agricultural traditions deeply influence my life and work. Through the UK Cooperative Extension Service, I’ve been able to contribute to the state’s communities.
